sentences.text: Yeah, the--
No, no.
No, no, no.
Absolutely no.

We had a-- we had an idea that something quite horrible has been happening to our relatives, but we-- we didn't know what, and we still had hope that maybe after the war or somehow-- yeah, the war, and maybe we'll see some of them.
We didn't have a complete idea.
We didn't-- news was coming from time to time, but-- but I think the political circles of-- in the world knew.
They-- there were-- there were people who-- who were given complete information.
A Polish nobleman, who are very noble people, and they-- and they carried information to-- to Rome, to the pope, and to Mr.
Churchill, and also to Mr.
Roosevelt.
But obviously, nothing has been done.
Yeah.
The Polish people did inform-- around in the world-- it was-- carried the news, but nothing was done.
No.
We knew-- we didn't-- we didn't have the horrible news, no.
We didn't have the horrible details.
We didn't meet anybody who escaped, not at that time.
No.
OK, so we were at the--

Yes, that was the-- and as-- as I mentioned, General [INAUDIBLE], who was the leading person of the Polish underground and party, and the-- the-- the idea was a right idea, because the Germans were very weakened.
The Russians, having been defeated-- have-- have-- have had-- defeat-- defeated and won the battle of Stalingrad and pulling out from Ukraine and from-- from Russia, being victorious at that time.
Covering [INAUDIBLE], the army was practically running for the revenge to Berlin and [? trusting ?] Poland.
And-- so they thought this is the right time.
And indeed, it was.
But apparently the Germans, in the last effort, just before they knew they will collapse, they-- they made this terrific effort.
And they won.
It was not difficult to-- to win over the Polish revolutionaries, who were not [? too-- ?] well supplied with the-- with the arms like the-- like the Warsaw ghetto was not supplied with arms.
They were not-- they were-- the command was-- I think it was as certain-- maybe specially purposely, the information was not-- not quite right.
And-- and especially, the-- the devious Russian attitude of stopping at Praga, when they could in a few minutes be in a war zone finish the whole.
Deviously stopped in Praga and let the Polish revolutionaries bled-- bleed to death.
We were in the shelters.
We were tearing the-- the-- the-- sheets for bandages.
There was not enough food.
There was nothing at the hospital, there were not-- not enough nurses.
There was not enough-- disorganized.
And all because the Polish people thought--
--once they started the revolution, the Russians would come in together, they liberate-- the Poles liberate Warsaw in their own right, in their own-- and then together they will defeat the Germans.
But it proved to be different.
The Germans were still quite strong.
They didn't-- were not strong, but they put a resistance.
They-- the revolution kept-- I mean, nearly eight weeks, completely defeated.
A lot of people lost lives.
And we were in the shelters.
And we were very lucky, because one day young German soldiers arrived-- soldier arrived.
He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And [INAUDIBLE] were in the-- the children were-- they were starving, so we used to go out of the shelter.
We young, I and other-- my friend [?
Masia ?] and somebody else-- and we went in search for bread for this children.
And as we went for search for the [INAUDIBLE], da da da da da, we could be killed any time.
But young people, who cared?
Also, this cousin of my friend and fiance, [?
Julie-- ?] suddenly after a long, you know, walk here and there through Warsaw, joined us and this-- came to [INAUDIBLE]-- to our place where we were, and he joined us in this shelter.
So we were three together.
And then we went out.
We joined an enormous queue of horrible looking people, all like us.
Dirty and starving, and their faces looking like God knows what, like out of, you know, Dante's hell.
And then we joined the queue, and the Russian and the Germans, again with the rifles and the uniforms, and the German order.
And we started walking through Warsaw.
And Warsaw, you must remember, in-- before the war, it was the most charming, delightful city.
A small Paris.
Full of cafes, full of beautiful houses, full of shops.
Full of elegant women.
Full of-- really, truly charming places-- theaters, and concerts, and it was quite a cultured place, and it was all burned down.
The Germans systematically burned every single house.
And we were walking in the middle of the road, and it was-- it was a picture of hell.
I don't know how hell could look.
Maybe look it looks nice by comparison what we have seen.
It was like the end of the world.
Eventually, they brought us to a railway station-- I don't even know which one it was-- and boarded the trains.
And I remember saying to my friend, [?
Masia, ?] I said-- oh yes, on their way to the trains, they stopped us.
And they took all of the men away to a church.
All the men.
Stefan, and I-- we thought we'd never see him again.
We thought they're going to burn them down in a church.
And then all the women and children boarded the train.
And we arrived at a place called Pruszkow, which again, was about half an hour away from Warsaw.
And it was a detention camp.
So we arrived there, and it was an enormous, enormous square.
And the end of the square was sort of barracks.
It must have been, before the war, must've been the soldiers' barracks.
And-- and-- and there we went with masses of people.
The floors, I remember, were very beautiful floors-- oak floors, but everything was slippery with food, and we had to queue for hours to get soup.
And the soup they gave us, very, very flat.
Very flat plate, so when you-- they put the [INAUDIBLE], the soup on this, immediately it slipped over and you had nothing.
And we were slipping on this-- on this floor [INAUDIBLE].
And full of vermin already, after a week, full of vermin.
And what was-- the procedure was like this.
There were a lot of old and lot of lame people-- or people-- invalids.
The-- the Polish nurses used to wait on the square while they were being discharged daily by the German command.
They were-- they made this place-- especially because next to it was a big hospital.
So all these people with-- unable to-- I mean, people-- invalids with-- and old were taken to this hospital by the Polish nurses, Red Cross nurses, and left there.
After [INAUDIBLE] they got an injection, and-- and they got rid of them.
But the young were taken to different camps.
To extermination camps or to-- or to working place, or to the motherland to work for the war efforts.
So the selection took place every day at a certain hour, and the big lorry came.
The Germans came, and all too familiar for me, for us.
And taking the young people, a cousin of mine, went to Belsen-Bergen this way and another friend went to another camp.
And as we have watched the procedure of these things, we always retreated to the toilets, and we pretended to be busy and cleaning there.
And each time it took her like an hour or half hour, we came back.
And so it went for a long time.

And after maybe a week or two weeks or 10 days, two the Red Cross nurses approached us, and-- not knowing what to do we-- the two of us were Jewish, and said, look we want-- we are saving young people.
We want to save you.
And meanwhile, we befriended a girl called Krystyna, who was a Polish girl.
And we want to befriend-- we want to save you.
How?
We'll put-- place the uniforms, Red Cross uniforms and caps, and-- and our identity cards in the pocket under the-- under the bench in the toilets.
And you dress into it, go into big square, take who is coming out, go past the gate.
Remember-- memorize the name, your name, your name which should be like the nurse, and then go to the left and take to the disabled person to the hospital and come-- and disappear.
As luck would have it, my friend [?
Masia ?] had great friends in Pruszkow who owed a pharmacy.
And you know, it-- it seems to you probably completely like a fabricated story.
But this pharmacy was opposite this camp, and she said, I know this place.
It has been for me many times-- this was a Polish girl who-- whose-- who parents and her husband run the pharmacy.
And she said, look, if-- if it is what I think, in any case, let meet-- let's meet opposite on the stairs.
Opposite this-- opposite this camp.
She told us the number, which would-- if it should be this number, this is the pharmacy.
I'm almost sure.
She inquired before if there is such [INAUDIBLE], and she found out there is such a pharmacy.
So she was almost sure.
We asked the nurses, that there is a third girl who wants-- we want her to be saved with us.
So first went [?
Masia ?] and disappeared.
Second went I.
I put the uniform.
Believe me, I couldn't remember the name.
I couldn't remember anything.
Nothing at all registered in my name.
I went into this enormous square-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --went into the enormous square-- and-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --maybe somebody want-- maybe my daughter.
So I should stop now.
No, no, no.
I want to this is enormous square-- and-- yes, in the kitchen-- and she-- and I was waiting for somebody, some invalid to come out.
Now-- now, look at the series of coincidences.
Now, where we lived on [INAUDIBLE] Street in Warsaw, it was very prominent and right in the elegant [INAUDIBLE].
That was the heart of the town.
It was a very elegant street.
There, on the corner, we-- we lived.
It was a big pharmacy-- very elegant, and the-- the house was, you know, 18th or 19th century, beautiful house.
And there was a niche.
It was a very big, beautiful pharmacy and old fashioned.
And in the niche stood always-- for years, I remember him-- a blind man.
And he had on his neck here suspended on a little leather-- slip of leather-- a tray, and he was selling cigarettes.
And he had a dog and a white stick, and he was blind.
And I remember this man, you know, [? like. ?] And I stay in this, entered this big square.
No one is coming to be taken to this hospital.
And I am-- and I am absolutely frightened to death what is going to happen.
Where-- where-- when would somebody come?
And they were all the time released by the German doctors or whatever.
And as I stay in this big, enormous square, and just walking, look at the sky, this blind man appears.
This blind man appears without the dog, without this strip, without out the cigarettes, but the [? blind ?] with the white stick.
I run up to him and I say, I know you.
I know you.
I know you all my life.
You were at [INAUDIBLE].
He said, yes.
I said, don't worry.
Don't be afraid.
I'm going to take you to the hospital.
I take him this-- under the arm.
And as slow as it possibly can be, I take him to the gate, not knowing the name, not knowing anything, past the gate with the smiling, holding him under the arm, and go right into the hospital.
I deposited him there, took away my cap, because the girls there were already involved in that plot.
They saved about maybe 100 girls like that.
And run across to the pharmacy on the stairs.
Afterwards, I got to know from Stefan, who joined us after the whole ordeal, that they discovered the plot and the shot maybe 30 nurses.
I was very, very upset about that story.
The Germans discovered that.
And we found ourselves-- also Krystyna-- on the stairs.
The pharmacy was closed to our great regret and anxiety.
So we decided to stay there.
But we fell on our knees, and I speak of believing or not believe-- believe me, all we said is, God Almighty, thank you.
Thank you.
God Almighty, Father in heaven, thank you.
You know, couldn't-- the gratefulness was so great.
We were so delighted.
And after midnight or so, this young man appeared and [?
Masia ?] ventured to see him.
He opened the door.
And he-- he thought he sees a ghost from-- from the-- from the-- from the ground.
And then-- and then two of us appeared, filthy, full of vermin, smelling.
He said, God Almighty, I remember you.
He said, sweet Jesus, God Almighty.
He just couldn't believe what has befallen him.
And he ushered us to his place, and we told him the story.
He said his wife and his father, parents-in-law are on a holiday.
Anyway, he gave us shelter for three days.
For three days, he was pouring-- I remember in Polish it was called [POLISH].
And he poured over us stuff to kill the vermin.
We were full of lice, full-- full of it.
He-- we burnt-- he-- he burnt our dresses.
He gave us dresses of his wife.
He gave us food, he gave us money.
You know, all Polish people help.
He knew [?
Masia-- ?] she was his friend--
Masia ?] he knew, yeah.
Yes, she was [INAUDIBLE].
No, no.
It was a young Polish woman.
And he told us that we should not go by-- travel by-- by trains because it's dangerous, there we can be approached by the Germans.
We should go through the lanes to this place called [INAUDIBLE], which was-- Krystyna's mother was there.
And Krystyna's uncle was a rich farmer.
So it was like 120 kilometers from Warsaw.
So we embarked on walking by foot.
After a short time, we had to walk barefoot because-- because the shoes were torn to pieces.
So what we did, we worked early morning till about noon or so.
Then we stopped at the farm which we thought looks nice.
We approached the peasants.
The peasants in Poland were very nice, unlike the lower middle class, which was [INAUDIBLE] to the nuns, the peasants were fabulous.
They received, as there were a lot of refugees from Warsaw.
There was no question of Jewish anymore at all.
So they received us very warmly.
They gave us a lot of food.

But we had to work in the fields, so we learned how to work in the field-- how to dig potatoes, how to-- cucumber, and tomatoes, and cabbage-- endless, endless rows of cabbage, and our back was breaking and we helped.
Like that we were slowly going forward and forward.
They were f Sometimes we were given a little lift by a cart, and more than often, we-- we had to walk from one farm, from one village, from one village-- 120 kilometers.
But there were advantages, because sometimes we went to a farm.
There was a brook.
They-- we worked the day-- the half of the day always-- then was a brook.
We had a swim and bath in the brook.
Then we-- then we slept in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE] stables on hay.
High stacks of it.
And the hay was-- you know, hay's so beautifully smelly.
Got right into the head.
We were just lying there-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
Somebody's at the door now.
Can you open the door, kindly?
And we fell asleep in that way.
And in this way, we reached [INAUDIBLE].
And when we reached-- but we also had all kinds of-- there was a priest among us, and so on and so forth-- there's a lot of stories.
Eventually we reach [INAUDIBLE], and to our dismay and discomfort and absolute fright, there were Wehrmacht.
The German soldiers were digging trenches all over the place.
They were already nearly defeated.
But-- I want to take you back to Pruszkow.
You should open the door.
Pruszkow.
There was about-- about three, four days.
No more.
He was afraid.
He provided-- he made-- he cleansed us completely.
He provided new clothes and some food and some money for us.
And he-- he let us walk.
I-- I meant the-- the camp, the--
Oh, about two weeks altogether, altogether.
About two weeks.
Not too long.
No, no, no.
A transit camp.
Only transit camp.
Nothing-- all kinds of people.
Nothing-- all kinds of people.
Nothing to do with  Jewish.
That was-- that was a transit camp actually for selection to-- to working in the fatherland, motherland, or to-- to send us to-- to-- to certain-- certain camps for examination.
Polish people were also exterminated.
About one million people of Polish people were exterminated one way or the other.
Didn't like somebody, [INAUDIBLE] like this.
So that was that.
That was-- that was a lovely time, because we walked among-- it was not-- somehow you didn't see a German.
Here and there, you saw the passing of motorcycles.
They didn't bother about you.
And apart from our sore feet, we work-- and sore hands-- we work in the fields.
We learned a lot how to-- you know, how to get the hay and-- and straw and God know what.
Than we had always a nice meal.
And at one occasion, it was very, very funny because, you see, my friend comes from a very religious, Jewish home.
But she came from a very Orthodox home.
And one-- of one of the occasions, you were obliged to go to the-- obliged to go to the church, because, I mean, they-- they-- they thought-- I said to [?
Masia, ?] look.
We have to go to-- Krystyna goes to the church.
I will have to go to-- so she [INAUDIBLE], say I'm ill.
So we did like that.
Every time we said, she's ill.
I didn't mind to go to the church, because I always believe it's one God.
I believe in-- I admired, and I thought our ethics, our Jewish ethics, and our religion is-- is one.
We have given the Ten Commandments to the whole world, which is, up [? today ?] Romans and-- and-- and-- and the whole modern world took [? out a ?] [?
God ?].
I believed in that.
I was proud of being that, but I believe this one God.
So it didn't matter whether I prayed in the church or whatever.
So [?
Masia ?] was always ill.
On one occasion, after the church was a big feast.
And the-- the farmer's wife, one of the farmer's wife, made a big feast.
And it is prevalent in the-- in the-- in the Polish gastronomic sort of kitchen.
And the kitchen, a gastronomic recipes.
A soup is made of goose blood.
Goose blood?
Blood.
Blood.
They do it to all kinds of things, and it's actually very good.
We didn't know about it, and we were always hungry, young people, so we ate the soup with goose blood and God knows what afterwards.
And afterwards, we found out that Krystyna told me, this is goose blood.
Masia, ?] I remember, she never went out of the toilet for maybe six hours.
She will sick and sick and sick and sick till she was completely dehydrated.
It didn't harm me somehow.
But she was completely dehydrated.
We felt very, very, very unpleasant.
Because blood, in our religion, is one thing we mustn't eat.
We drain our meat from blood.
And blood-- and there-- she's-- she said she's contaminated for-- for life.
Anyway, we worked there, and we helped the-- the-- the lady, the farmer's wife.
No, eventually we reach Bobrovnik, yes, in a grand style because at the end, we found a-- a priest who was going to-- to marry somebody, and he was going with the horse and cart.
And he saw us poor girls working, so he invited us to this-- to-- also to this wedding.
And we-- we-- we-- we went with him to the wedding.
We did go to the actual wedding because we were not dressed properly.
But we were at this very wealthy farm.
And then he was going to Bobrovnik, and we arrived in great style.
And then we found out that were are in the middle of German milieu again.
And this army, military-- and we were scared, scared, scared to death-- the two of us, you know?
Krystyna disappeared because she was with her family, and we working in the kitchens.
And then Stefan appears also, because through the pharmacy, he came to-- was taken to Pruszkow.
He went to the pharmacy.
He-- he knew where we were because we left the dress.
He came after you.
Or--
And he came to us, to meet us.
And he was there.
And once or twice we had a scare because there were some people, musicians, who were in band.
And you know, there were a lot of musicians in Poland, and they mixed [INAUDIBLE] the Jewish people.
And they thought [?
Masia ?] and I, and we were a bit scared.
But Stefan said, they are already losing their war, don't worry.
And they were.
And indeed, you see, one day-- and it was-- we were one day-- one year, and more and more and more refugees came from Warsaw, and young people.
And we had a very, very jolly time at times, apart from hard work.
And we supported Stefan.

Whenever we went to the [INAUDIBLE] to cook, we had aprons with the-- with the-- with the big pockets inside, so whenever we cut a piece of sausage, cut a piece of meat, cut a piece of that-- right in.
And then we came home, we fed Stefan, because Stefan was [INAUDIBLE].
He cooked for himself.
And then we went--
Yeah, then-- then the lady, or the farmer's wife, was a very fine and very nice lady.
And the farmer himself was really a gentleman, a Polish gentleman.
Polish farmer gentleman.
He was not a peasant, he was a farmer.
He owed a lot of land.
And then we were told we are going to be dispersed and given huts to live with peasants.
And we were given a hut.
A very, very, very nice man he was.
His name was Kunat, Mr.
Kunat.
And he was a very clever man.
A very clever man.
And we settled in his hut.
But part of the hut was not finished.
The floor was-- there was sand on the floor.
So it was not finished, and this part we were given.
So, you know, you ate with the sand, you slept with the sand.
So were three of us, and we all were-- we all had, you know, one big, iron bed.
And we had an iron stove.
And we were-- what we could steal from the farmer's wife, [INAUDIBLE] then we had later.
Then, in addition-- so we-- we couldn't live with this sand.
So without the knowledge of this poor farmer, we put water under us, so to make it a little-- and then when winter came, you know, big mushrooms grew in the corners, enormous mushrooms.
It was frightening.
And somehow-- then we were not given so much food.
So what we were given-- you know, Stefan was made-- Stefan was very much liked by the peasant-- by the-- by the-- by Mr.
Kunat.
Because Stefan was an agricultural engineer.
Finished one of the high schools in Warsaw.
So he gave him advice about the earth.
He told him when to put what and how to live the earth in piece.
Finished.
OK, we're coming out at the end of the tape six.
How many tapes more he has?
